original_abstract:
The coordination of carbon monoxide to metal centers and the resulting shift in its stretching frequency ($\nu_{CO}$) are central themes in organometallic chemistry, traditionally interpreted through the Dewar–Chatt–Duncanson (DCD) model. In this work, the relationship between $\sigma$ donation, $\pi$ back-donation, and the electrostatic effects of the metal-ligand fragment is quantitatively investigated across a broad range of more than 30 complexes. These include a systematic series of $[(L)Au(CO)]^{0/+}$ gold(i) carbonyls as well as homoleptic anionic, neutral, and cationic metal carbonyls. Using charge-displacement (CD) analysis, the authors decompose the electron density flow into $\sigma$ and $\pi$ components to provide unambiguous measures of charge transfer and CO polarization.

The findings reveal that while $\sigma$ donation remains relatively constant across the various systems, $\pi$ back-donation is highly variable and serves as the primary driver of the spectroscopic CO response. Specifically, the $\pi$ component of charge rearrangement correlates strictly with changes in CO bond length, stretching frequency shifts, and the direction of CO $\pi$-polarization. This analysis demonstrates that $\pi$ back-donation is a significant factor even in "non-classical" carbonyls, where the CO bond is strengthened or blue-shifted. By providing a unified framework that accounts for both covalent and electrostatic contributions, this study enables the quantitative extraction of $\pi$-donor properties of metal-ligand moieties from experimental spectroscopic data.
humanized_abstract:
CO activation in metal carbonyls has been extensively investigated both theoretically and experimentally in organometallic chemistry, the difference in CO vibrational frequencies often attributed exclusively to the influence of the Dewar–Chatt–Duncanson (DCD) model within the framework of the qualitative understanding of organometallic systems. Here we investigate quantitatively through the whole spectrum of organometallic carbon monoxide activation the relationship between the $\sigma$ and $\pi$ components of the electron density flowing through the ligand to metal bond and the metal-centre electrostatic effects for a wide variety of metal complexes encompassing more than thirty systems distributed across several model sets amongst which we have homoleptic anionic, neutral and cationic transition metal complexes, and the full range of $[(L)Au(CO)]^{0/+}$ gold(i) carbonyls studied experimentally to analyse CO coordination by charge-displacement analysis, disclosing for the first time a clear distinction between charge transfer and CO polarisation.

Charge rearrangement mechanisms for transition metal complexes probed by CO spectroscopyIn the previously published work, it was established that CO stretching frequency shift is determined by the sum of the σ-donation and π-back-donation occurring from the metal complex to the CO ligand.To provide a clear understanding of the magnitude of the different charge rearrangement components, the values of σ and π were plotted for the five different transition metal systems. As seen from the plots, the value of σ remains fairly constant for all five complexes while that of π is vastly different and dominates the CO response in the spectroscopic measurements. Most importantly, a quantitative correlation was demonstrated between the value of π and all CO stretches measured in this study: i) the change in the CO bond length, ii) the wavenumber shift, and iii) the direction of CO π-polarization. Hence, π-back-donation is an important factor also for the non-classical carbonyls where the CO bond is stretched or red-shifted. Overall, this work provides the first general and quantitative model, that unifies the covalent and electrostatic aspects of the interactions between the metal complex and the CO ligand, and allows the π-donor character of the ML fragment to be quantitatively determined from spectroscopic measurements.

document.input:
The coordination of carbon monoxide to metal centers and the resulting shift in its stretching frequency ($\nu_{CO}$) are central themes in organometallic chemistry, traditionally interpreted through the Dewar–Chatt–Duncanson (DCD) model. In this work, the relationship between $\sigma$ donation, $\pi$ back-donation, and the electrostatic effects of the metal-ligand fragment is quantitatively investigated across a broad range of more than 30 complexes. These include a systematic series of $[(L)Au(CO)]^{0/+}$ gold(i) carbonyls as well as homoleptic anionic, neutral, and cationic metal carbonyls. Using charge-displacement (CD) analysis, the authors decompose the electron density flow into $\sigma$ and $\pi$ components to provide unambiguous measures of charge transfer and CO polarization.

The findings reveal that while $\sigma$ donation remains relatively constant across the various systems, $\pi$ back-donation is highly variable and serves as the primary driver of the spectroscopic CO response. Specifically, the $\pi$ component of charge rearrangement correlates strictly with changes in CO bond length, stretching frequency shifts, and the direction of CO $\pi$-polarization. This analysis demonstrates that $\pi$ back-donation is a significant factor even in "non-classical" carbonyls, where the CO bond is strengthened or blue-shifted. By providing a unified framework that accounts for both covalent and electrostatic contributions, this study enables the quantitative extraction of $\pi$-donor properties of metal-ligand moieties from experimental spectroscopic data.
